notes.en: Warm, dry weather — classic up-country wet/intermediate zone cabbage-growing season conditions (Nuwara Eliya, Badulla). Short generation time (14–21 days) and high insecticide-resistance potential.

description.en: The key global pest of cabbage and other brassicas; one of the most significant cabbage pests in Sri Lanka's up-country cabbage-growing areas. Larvae window-feed on young leaves, producing irregular holes and a skeletonized appearance; heavy infestation reduces head quality and marketability. **High insecticide-resistance risk** — farmers over-relying on chemicals has driven resistance. **Sri Lanka IPM research** (Tropical Agricultural Research & Extension, 2022) evaluates physical barriers (nets, covers) as eco-friendly alternatives. Key management: (a) crop rotation away from brassicas; (b) trap crops (mustard attractants); (c) biocontrol — *Trichogramma chilonis* (egg parasitoid), *Cotesia plutellae* (larval parasitoid), *Chrysoperla carnea* (lacewing predator); (d) selective insecticides — chlorantraniliprole, spinosad, B. thuringiensis (Bt); avoid chlorpyrifos + pyrethroids in rotation to delay resistance; (e) physical exclusion nets on nursery beds and early-growth crops.

description.quote: in Sri Lanka, diamondback moth is one of the key pests of cabbage that causes significant yield loss, with farmers heavily using agro-chemicals to control it while ignoring alternative non-chemical eco-friendly pest management strategies. Research has evaluated the combined effectiveness of physical pest management strategies to suppress the diamondback moth population in cabbage as eco-friendly alternatives.

favored_conditions.quote: Common natural enemies of the moth include the parasitoids Trichogramma chilonis and Cotesia plutella and the predator Chrysoperla carnea, a lacewing
